description: 10 pre-1940 public land historical sites suitable for metal detecting

locations.name: Monte Cristo Ghost Town
locations.description: Silver and gold mining ghost town established in 1893 in the North Cascades of Snohomish County. At its peak Monte Cristo had 2,000 residents, a concentrator mill, and rail connection to Everett. The mines produced millions in silver, gold, copper, and lead before closure around 1907. The historic townsite and mill ruins are preserved within the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest on US Forest Service land, accessible via a scenic 4-mile trail along the Sauk River.
locations.source: USFS Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest - Public Land
locations.notes: Monte Cristo Ghost Town - USFS Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF, open public access, Snohomish County, WA - metal detecting generally allowed on USFS land

locations.name: Nighthawk Townsite
locations.description: Gold mining camp established around 1900 in the Okanogan Highlands near the Canadian border in Okanogan County. The Nighthawk area produced gold from placer and hard-rock mines, with multiple stamp mills and a railroad spur connecting to Oroville. The townsite ruins and surrounding mining landscape occupy Bureau of Land Management land in the Similkameen River valley, freely accessible for exploration.
locations.notes: Nighthawk Townsite - BLM Washington managed, open to public, Okanogan County, WA - metal detecting allowed on BLM land

locations.name: Fort Okanogan State Park
locations.description: Site of the Pacific Fur Company's Fort Okanogan, established in 1811 by David Stuart as the first permanent American settlement in present-day Washington State. Located at the confluence of the Okanogan and Columbia Rivers, the post was later operated by the North West Company (1813) and Hudson's Bay Company (1821) until 1847. The interpretive site is managed by Washington State Parks at the edge of Lake Pateros (Columbia River reservoir) with publicly accessible grounds.
locations.notes: Fort Okanogan State Park - WA State Parks managed, open to public, near Brewster, WA - state park, verify detecting permit requirements

locations.name: Conconully Historic Townsite
locations.description: Silver mining camp and former Okanogan County seat established in 1888 during the Okanogan Highlands silver rush. Conconully briefly flourished with hotels, saloons, and a courthouse before silver prices collapsed in 1893. The historic townsite sits adjacent to Conconully State Park along Salmon Creek, with Washington State Parks managing the surrounding public land and reservoir area.
locations.notes: Conconully Historic Townsite - WA State Parks / public land, open to public, Okanogan County, WA - verify detecting permit at state park

locations.name: Fort Simcoe State Park Historic Site
locations.description: U.S. Army post built in 1856 in the heart of Yakama territory following the Yakima War. Converted to an Indian agency in 1859 and became the Yakama Indian Agency headquarters. Five original buildings survive including the commandant's house. Washington State Parks, publicly accessible grounds.
locations.notes: Fort Simcoe State Park Historic Site - WA State Parks, open to public, near White Swan, WA

locations.name: Steptoe Battlefield State Park
locations.description: Site of the May 16-17, 1858 Battle of Steptoe, where a U.S. Army column under Lieutenant Colonel Edward Steptoe was surrounded and forced to retreat by Spokane, Palouse, and Coeur d'Alene warriors. One of the most significant U.S. Army defeats in the Pacific Northwest. Washington State Parks, open grounds with monument.
locations.notes: Steptoe Battlefield State Park - WA State Parks, public access, near Rosalia, WA

locations.name: Liberty Ghost Town
locations.description: Gold placer mining camp established in the 1880s and booming by 1893, Liberty is one of the oldest and most intact gold mining ghost towns in Washington. The Swauk Mining District produced gold continuously into the early twentieth century. Located on Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest land with public access to historic mining structures and placer claims.
locations.source: USFS Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest - Public Land
locations.notes: Liberty Ghost Town - USFS Okanogan-Wenatchee NF, public access, near Cle Elum, WA

locations.name: Ruby Ghost Town
locations.description: Silver and gold mining boom town established in 1888 in the Okanogan Highlands that briefly had 1,500 residents, two hotels, and a newspaper. The mines produced silver, gold, and copper until the collapse of silver prices in 1893. Located on Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest land, townsite and mining ruins are publicly accessible.
locations.source: USFS Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest - Public Land
locations.notes: Ruby Ghost Town - USFS Okanogan NF, public access, near Conconully, WA

locations.name: Cape Disappointment State Park (Fort Canby)
locations.description: Lewis and Clark reached the Pacific Ocean near this headland in November 1805, establishing a temporary camp at present-day Cape Disappointment. Fort Canby was built here in 1864 to defend the Columbia River mouth. Two historic lighthouses, the oldest in Washington (1856), stand on the cape. Washington State Parks with extensive public beach and trail access.
locations.notes: Cape Disappointment State Park - WA State Parks, open to public, near Ilwaco, WA

locations.name: Fort Nisqually Historic Site
locations.description: Hudson's Bay Company trading post established in 1833 as the first European settlement on Puget Sound. Fort Nisqually was a major agricultural and fur-trading center serving the southern Puget Sound region for decades. Two original HBC granary buildings dating to 1843 and 1850 survive and were moved to Point Defiance Park. City of Tacoma public park, freely accessible.
locations.source: City of Tacoma / Metro Parks - Public Land
locations.notes: Fort Nisqually Historic Site - City of Tacoma Metro Parks, open to public, Point Defiance Park, Tacoma, WA
